segments.text: Welcome back, today we're going to talk about pivot points.
Specifically the way I trade them on the 60-min chart.
But first, a quick word from our sponsor, BrokerCo.
BrokerCo gives you commission-free trading and a free month for new sign-ups.
OK, back to pivots. A pivot is the prior period's high, low, and close averaged.
We use it as a key intraday level. Above pivot, we're long-biased.
Below pivot, short-biased. The setup I trade is called the Pullback Hold.
Entry: stock opens above pivot, pulls back to pivot in the first hour, holds.
Volume confirms - we want to see the pullback on declining volume.
Stop: 1 ATR below the pivot. Target: prior day's high.
Let's look at NVDA on March 5th - classic Pullback Hold setup.
Opened at 850, pivot was 845, pulled back to 845.50 by 10:30.
I entered at 846 with stop at 843 and target 858 - prior day high.
Closed at 857.50 a few hours later. About 11 R-multiples on the trade.
Now, the most important thing - and people miss this - is discipline.
If you don't journal every trade, you can't see your real edge.
Question from chat: what do you do if the pullback is on rising volume?
Great question. Rising volume on the pullback means I skip the trade.
It tells me sellers are still active and the level may not hold.
Alright, that's it for today. Subscribe and I'll see you tomorrow.
